title: Specialty Coffee Water Mineralization Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Optimal Water Mineralization for Specialty Coffee
description: Calculate water hardness and mineralization for specialty coffee. SCA standards: TDS 75-250 ppm, hardness 50-175 mg/L, pH 6.5-7.5. Optimize your brewing.
intro: **Water is 98% of your brewed cup of coffee**. Its mineral composition dramatically affects taste: **magnesium** extracts bright citric notes, **calcium** adds body, **bicarbonate** neutralizes acidity. The **SCA (Specialty Coffee Association)** defines the ideal range: 150 ppm TDS, total hardness 50–175 mg/L CaCO₃. This calculator evaluates your current water and suggests adjustments.
keyTakeaway: **SCA target**: TDS 75–250 ppm, hardness 50–175 mg/L, pH 6.5–7.5, bicarbonate 40–75 mg/L. Distilled water is worse than hard water; minerals are essential.
useCases: Your coffee tastes flat or metallic—you suspect the water.
You're buying bottled water and want to know which brand works.
You have a water filter and want to optimize it.
You make specialty coffee and want to compete on quality.
Your espresso channeling looks off and you suspect mineralization issues.

example.title: Example: Buenos Aires Tap Water
steps: **Result**: Buenos Aires tap water is suitable for specialty coffee.
example.result: Water within SCA range. Filter with a Brita pitcher to remove chlorine before brewing.

explanation:
## Why Water Matters

Coffee is **98.5% water** in a brewed cup. Water's mineral composition **extracts different compounds** from the coffee:

- **Magnesium (Mg²⁺)**: extracts citric and malic acids (brightness).
- **Calcium (Ca²⁺)**: extracts sweet compounds and body.
- **Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)**: buffer, neutralizes acidity.
- **Sodium**: doesn't aid extraction, adds salty flavor.

## SCA Standards (Specialty Coffee Association)

| Parameter | Target Range | Critical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **TDS** | **150 ppm** | 75–250 |
| Total hardness | 50–175 mg/L CaCO₃ | 50–175 |
| Calcium hardness | 17–85 mg/L | 17–85 |
| Alkalinity | 40–75 mg/L | 40–75 |
| **pH** | **7.0** | 6.5–7.5 |
| Chlorine | 0 mg/L | 0 |
| Odor | Clean | – |

## Commercial Bottled Water for Coffee

| Brand | TDS | pH | Good for Coffee? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volvic | 130 | 7.0 | ✓ excellent |
| Evian | 357 | 7.2 | ✗ over-mineralized |
| Villavicencio | 240 | 7.5 | ✓ good |
| Villa del Sur | 85 | 7.1 | ✓ good |
| Home mineralized water | Varies | 7–8 | ✓ good |
| Distilled water | 0 | 5.5 | ✗ won't extract |
| Buenos Aires tap water | 150–200 | 7–7.5 | ✓ if filtered |

See also [espresso TDS and yield](/espresso-tds-yield) and [coffee ratio for V60 pour over](/coffee-ratio-v60-pour-over).

faq.q: Is distilled water bad for coffee?
faq.a: Yes. It has no minerals to extract the coffee. The brew tastes flat without brightness or body. Add minerals (Third Wave Water) for better results.

faq.q: Can you use tap water for specialty coffee?
faq.a: It depends on your location. In some cities tap water is excellent (just filter for chlorine). In areas with very hard water, you may need to filter or blend it.

faq.q: Is a Brita filter enough for specialty coffee?
faq.a: It removes chlorine and some hardness, but doesn't restructure minerals. For specialty coffee, reverse osmosis plus remineralization is ideal.

faq.q: Does Third Wave Water work for specialty coffee?
faq.a: Yes. Their mineral packets are specifically designed for coffee (Classic and Espresso profiles). Mix with distilled water and you're ready to brew.

faq.q: Does hot brewing water affect mineral composition?
faq.a: Not significantly in the normal brewing range (90–96°C). The mineral composition remains stable.

faq.q: Is it worth optimizing water for regular coffee?
faq.a: For filter coffee: yes, the difference is noticeable. For regular supermarket coffee: less critical. For high-end specialty: definitely worth it.

faq.q: What's the best water pH for brewing coffee?
faq.a: SCA recommends 6.5–7.5, with 7.0 as ideal. This pH range allows balanced extraction of acids and sweetness.

faq.q: How do I know if my water is too hard or too soft?
faq.a: Too hard (>175 mg/L): coffee tastes dull, over-extracted. Too soft (<50 mg/L): coffee tastes sour, thin. Test with an inexpensive TDS meter.
